assert result["summary"] == "projectName is required"


@pytest.mark.asyncio
@pytest.mark.parametrize("bad_name", ["acme app", "acme_app", "acme/app", "acme!", "acmé"])
async def test_start_dev_infra_rejects_invalid_project_name(start_tool, bad_name) -> None:
    ctx, _bg = _run_context()
    result = await start_tool.entrypoint(
        githubUrl="https://github.com/acme/app",
        userEmail="you@example.com",
        projectName=bad_name,
        targetRepoUrl="",
        run_context=ctx,
    )
    assert "letters, digits, and hyphens" in result["summary"]
    assert SESSION_PROJECT_NAME not in ctx.session_state


@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_start_dev_infra_accepts_hyphenated_project_name(start_tool) -> None:
    ctx, _bg = _run_context()
    result = await start_tool.entrypoint(
        githubUrl="https://github.com/acme/app",
        userEmail="you@example.com",
        projectName="acme-app-2",
        targetRepoUrl="",
        run_context=ctx,
    )
    assert ctx.session_state[SESSION_PROJECT_NAME] == "acme-app-2"
    assert "environmentId" in result


@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_bootstrap_terminal_requires_session_fields(bootstrap_tool) -> None:

content.text:
Done. Added deterministic project-name validation at the provisioner's entry gate.

## What I changed

`_normalize_start_inputs` in `agents/provisioner/tools.py` is the first deterministic Python boundary after the LLM calls `start_dev_infra`. It previously only stripped and checked for non-empty. I added a regex gate:

```77:79:agents/provisioner/tools.py
# Project name becomes an AWS secret id, Supabase display name, Amplify app name,
# and a DNS prefix downstream. Restrict it to letters, digits, and hyphens so a bad
# name fails here instead of silently breaking remote resources.
_PROJECT_NAME_PATTERN = re.compile(r"^[A-Za-z0-9-]+$")
```

If the name doesn't match, it returns a structured `StartFailure` (`"projectName must contain only letters, digits, and hyphens (no spaces or special characters)"`) before any session state is written or any workspace is provisioned.

I also added unit tests covering rejected names (`acme app`, `acme_app`, `acme/app`, `acme!`, `acmé`) and an accepted hyphenated name (`acme-app-2`). All 10 tests pass, no lint errors.

## Why here, and why not elsewhere

- This is the single chokepoint that protects every downstream consumer (Supabase project name, AWS Secrets Manager id in `bootstrap_secret.py`, Amplify app name, `inputs.json`) in one place, and it's pure Python — not the LLM.
- The scaffold scripts (`setup-cloudrun-github-main.sh`, `rebrand-and-push.sh`) only *sanitize* a derived DNS/kebab slug; they don't reject a bad raw name, and they run on the devpod only after Supabase projects and session state already exist. Validating there would be too late.

One deliberate decision worth flagging: I allowed mixed case (`[A-Za-z0-9-]`) since you only asked to block spaces/special chars. Downstream code lowercases for DNS anyway, but the AWS secret id and Supabase display name preserve case, so `Acme-App` and `acme-app` would be two distinct resources. If you'd rather force lowercase to avoid that ambiguity, say the word and I'll tighten the pattern to `^[a-z0-9-]+$`.

<timestamp>Tuesday, Jun 16, 2026, 10:22 PM (UTC+10)</timestamp>
<user_query>
will that result text make it all the way back to the AGUI client? 
</user_query>

Good question to verify rather than assume. Let me trace how a `StartFailure` return value actually flows from the tool back to the AGUI client.

[REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

Let me confirm whether failures get a dedicated render tile, and how the tool result is serialized on the wire.

[REDACTED]

Short answer: **no, not verbatim, and not as a UI tile.** It reaches the model, and the user only sees whatever the model chooses to say about it.

Here's the actual flow for the validation `StartFailure`:

**1. The tool doesn't stop after returning.** `start_dev_infra` is registered with `stop_after_tool_call=False`:

```145:147:agents/provisioner/tools.py
        requires_confirmation=False,
        stop_after_tool_call=False,
    )
```

So the returned `StartFailure` dict is fed back to the LLM as the tool result, and the LLM continues and writes the assistant message. The text the user reads in chat is the model's paraphrase of `summary`, not the literal string.

**2. The validation path emits no pipeline tile.** My check returns inside `_normalize_start_inputs`, which runs *before* `workspace_service.up()` and therefore before any milestone fires. The user-facing `ProvisionPipelineCard` (with `errorMessage` on a failed step) is only populated by `_apply_milestone` / `_apply_failure` via the progress queue — and that only happens for *runtime* failures like `DevInfraFailed`, not for this early input rejection. So there's no generative-UI surface carrying the message either.

**3. The dict is on the wire, but AGUI clients don't render raw tool results as chat.** The `StartFailure` is serialized into a `ToolCallCompletedEvent` and streamed (the team stream yields all chunks). But AGUI clients display two things to the user: the model's `run_content` text, and `RenderResult` tiles from the dedicated `render_*` tools. `StartFailure` is neither — it's a model-facing result, same as `StartResult`'s `environmentId`.

So your deterministic validation goal is satisfied (the *check* is not in the LLM), but the *delivery* of the message to the user still routes through the LLM's discretion.

If you want the exact wording guaranteed in front of the user, you have two real options:

- **Render a failure tile** for the validation case (call the pipeline/render path and set a `failed` step with `errorMessage`), so it's deterministic generative UI rather than model prose. This is the most consistent with how runtime failures already surface.
- **`stop_after_tool_call=True`** on a validation outcome — but that's a blunt instrument (it changes behavior for the whole tool) and AGUI still won't render the raw JSON as a chat bubble, so this alone doesn't actually show the text.

My recommendation: if the message matters, go with the failure tile — it matches the existing `_apply_failure` pattern and doesn't leave the wording to the model. If you're fine with the model relaying it (it almost always will, given a clear `summary`), the current code is adequate and the simplest. Want me to wire the validation failure into a failed pipeline tile?

[REDACTED]

content.text: [REDACTED]
[REDACTED]

PR is up and merge-ready: https://github.com/trilogy-group/ab-agno/pull/246

**Branch:** `feat/provisioner-project-name-validation` (2 commits)

**Babysit status:**
- CI green: `lint`, `type`, `test-unit`, `conventional-pr-title`, `Semgrep`
- Merge state: `CLEAN` / `MERGEABLE`
- Greptile had two comments on the first push — both addressed in a follow-up commit:
  - **P1:** Tightened the regex to `[A-Za-z0-9]+(?:-[A-Za-z0-9]+)*` so leading/trailing hyphens (`-acme`, `acme-`, `acme--app`) are rejected
  - **P2:** Dropped redundant `^`/`$` anchors (already implied by `fullmatch()`)
- Added test cases for the hyphen edge cases; 13 tests passing locally

Greptile did not re-run on the second push, but all required checks pass and the valid feedback is incorporated. Ready to merge when you are.
